page: Vultr voc-g-40c-160gb-768s-amd is an Optimized Cloud Compute instance built on the x86_64 architecture. It features 40 AMD EPYC Milan physical cores running at a base frequency of 2.0 GHz with a shared CPU allocation. The system is equipped with 160.0 GB of system memory, providing a balanced 4.0 GB of RAM per core. Storage is provided by a 768 GB local NVMe SSD, and the instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address.

In benchmark evaluations, the server demonstrates strong processing capabilities. The stress-ng single-core CPU performance score of 3083.57 ranks in the top tier (top 10%), while the multi-core CPU performance score of 123693.48 is rated as strong (top 25%). The shared CPU allocation represents a potential performance tradeoff under sustained, concurrent multi-tenant workloads, though the high core count and local NVMe storage mitigate resource constraints.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, this instance offers a high density of compute cores and memory relative to its hardware class, making it a viable option for resource-intensive deployments without the premium of dedicated hardware. It is suitable for general-purpose workloads, multi-threaded applications, database hosting, and web serving environments that benefit from high-capacity local NVMe storage and balanced memory-to-CPU ratios.
description: Vultr voc-g-40c-160gb-768s-amd is an Optimized Cloud Compute instance featuring 40 AMD EPYC Milan physical cores operating at 2.0 GHz with a shared CPU allocation. It is configured with 160.0 GB of RAM, yielding a balanced 4.0 GB per core, and includes a 768 GB local NVMe SSD. In performance testing, the server achieves a top-tier stress-ng single-core score of 3083.57 and a strong multi-core score of 123693.48. While the shared CPU allocation introduces potential resource contention under continuous peak loads, the high core count and fast local storage provide excellent resource density. This instance is well-suited for database hosting, multi-threaded application deployments, and general-purpose enterprise workloads.
